And that's one way that our approach is very different than traditional coaching, is that we believe that no person is an island. But it's looking at what's going on with your client and then thinking instead of what needs to be removed, what needs to be stopped, what could be added in to strengthen to make the person happier, to make their relationships better just by adding something. And sometimes what you're adding has nothing to do with the presenting problem. So an example of this would be you're talking to a person who's presenting problem is their intimate relationship. It comes from, you know, it's actually an Ericksonian principle, which is it's much easier to add something to a behavior than to take it away. That's the easiest way to change behavior. And also as a coach, you'll often be talking to someone and they'll tell you about their challenges and their ambitions. So one SI strategy would be pretend strategy, meaning making a deliberate behavior which is otherwise looked at as unconscious or uncontrollable. So if a husband and a wife and the husband gets, has an anxiety attack, then he can pretend to have the anxiety attack and the wife can be with him and they can have all the, he can experience all the benefits of having an anxiety attack without actually having one. So then the other part, of course, of expanding the unit is finding key supportive relationships that help or that have helped in the past. It was somebody just lying there looking very sick and the ministers were late for where they had to go. Well so they watched to see what are the conditions that really changed the person's behavior. So one of them is that they're going to go teach a story about the good Samaritan and the importance of charity. And then the other thing would be that they were just told a story about someone who helped someone and they're seeing what will make this person... And the third thing is that they're just late. And it turns out that being late for their talk was the most important factor for whether they just step over the person who's lying there sick or whether they stop and help them. So it's all social context. So here are some examples of strategies we use to help people in expanding their possibilities. So one is the archetype strategy where we help the client to find the parts of themselves that are super wise or very strong like the warrior or magical or very soft and nurturing like the lover or we help them get grounded. What we're doing is we're taking these parts that are inside of all of us and we're having a conversation with them and asking for their opinions and then helping the different parts of a person have a conversation and get together, get integrated and focused on how to overcome whatever the client's question is. So before doing that strategy, and this is the one minute version of that strategy, we like to always ask, I like to always have my clients ask a question that they will ask all those different parts of themselves that same question. And then when they get an answer, we move on to another part. So future thinking, a question you can ask your client is how would a perfect day look for you? How would a great relationship feel or be? Brainstorming, you want to find out all the different opportunities which could come about if this behavior or this pattern was shifted. What are all the different possibilities or strange, wonderful things that could happen without that as an obstacle? And then metaphorical play. What could a problem turn into? So Mark reminded me of a session we had during one of our teachings where a woman presented, Mark I can't remember what the problem was. It was basically she had some kind of fear anxiety and you turned it in. So I asked her what that would look like or what that fear anxiety would be if it was an animal I think. And then whatever animal she said it would be, I asked her now if she had a magical wand and could just transform that into something else, what would her wand be able to do, what would it turn into? And the woman immediately turned it into something very beautiful and light like a butterfly that could easily move and fly away. And as soon as she did that she was then able to talk about that behavioral problem or fear that she had in a different way because she had just in her own real experience transformed it through this way of talking about it as a metaphor. Because it was a fear, it was a way she felt about it and when you change it through a metaphor you're changing the feeling. So you're actually changing the thing. Another example is we talked to a student I think it was a week ago, maybe two weeks ago, a mother of a four year old who was overwhelmed by the little girl's behavior where the mother was doing something and the child would interrupt her and if the mother would not be interrupted the child would scream and cry and then the mother had to console her and so on. And we suggested that the mother initiate that game, that the mother say let's play that I'm doing the laundry and you call me and I say I can't come now and then you pretend to cry and scream and then I come and I pretend to cry and scream and we're both screaming. Something like that. I don't remember the details. It's changing a mother-child relationship that are stuck in an unpleasant pattern and making it a game. Right, so that's creating the greater possibilities that the stuck pattern now becomes a playful happy pattern. Right, so what if every time you felt like fighting instead it made you realize that you felt very sexy? That would be another example. So what are the possibilities of the ways that we can work with our patterns or emotions or blocks to transform them? That's what this stage is all about. And very often the biggest problem with someone's pattern is that they feel like it's the only one. So I was like do I don't, I eat ice cream. It was fighting this one thing rather than seeing the possibilities, all the different ways of feeling better in that moment or all the different things that are possible. So just the option of having many choices is important there. Right, and you know talking about many choices, one of my favorite strategies for dealing with food cravings is the putting it off. So instead of denying someone that desire for the treat, you put it off until the next day. So you never say no I can't have that and this comes from the concept, Chloe and Mark you guys will remember the name of that kind of conscious eating or something like that. Where people fall into different types with their behavior problem around food. What helps most of these types is just to say tomorrow I can have that. And as soon as you've given yourself that ability to have that thing the next day, a huge energy is released of that craving or desire. So this is very different than using shame or willpower. Instead it's knowing that you can do that the next day. So that's something that you can apply to all different types of behaviors. That's great. It probably shifted to the anticipatory part of the brain that's pleasurable. So you're still looking forward to it. Right. Yeah, great. Okay, so seventh stage, the final one. Yes. So this is where we applied the seven SI stages to the second coaching path, which is helping person to achieve their outcome and their goals. And so the first stage is understanding what you need to understand. So when you're helping somebody with a goal or an outcome, for instance, their career, their weight, their health, there anything, and it usually involves kind of the participation of the external world or the physical world somehow, then you need to, what you want to understand is you want to help them with reverse engineering. So in other words, one of the biggest, one of the most important metaphors and tools for helping someone with an outcome or goal is thinking from the goal backwards. Like say that you've already achieved it, you're here. You know, if you wanted to go to Paris, okay, so we're in Paris now. How did we get to Paris? Okay, we got in a flight, right? And we got a hotel room. How did we get the flight in the hotel room? We earned the money, we scheduled it. So how do we earn the money, we scheduled it? So you're working backwards to all the things that would require, that are requirements to get to Paris, right? And so that's one of the things that you want to use as a goal. As a tool. As a tool in your first stage when working with that. Yeah. So when you want to understand a person. So you're creating a map for getting to the outcome or goal and you're doing that by working backwards from the location on the map that you get to your destination back to when you began on that map. Yeah. And you can be the client's hand and write it out for them. You can use large pieces of paper to plan out the journey. So the other thing you want to understand is what is in the way. So what are the obstacles? What are the other behaviors, beliefs, relationships, rules and fears that are in the way? And what's the truth in the client's self-criticism? So how, you want to assess the severity of a problem. Sometimes someone will say, you know what, I'm getting this business started and I just can't believe how I procrastinate. And for one person, it's that they haven't even done step one of starting a business. And for the other person, they've done 99% and they're beating themselves up because they are counting right. Right? And so you want to assess to see, well, how close is this person to the goal and not to the goal, but how are they close or how much action are they actually taking so far? So that would be the first stage of understanding. Great. So second stage reframe. A coach brings a client a new focus and context for their goal and outcome. So the purpose is to attain a goal but in a doable phases and steps. Remember we're talking about outcome and goal based client coaching with strategic intervention. So you look at the life cycle, career cycle, life stage, you look at the relationship cycle that your client's in. So for example, their goal might be to get married, but right now they're single. So what comes between being single and getting married? They may want to have a great relationship with a teenage child, but now they're fighting five times a day. What goes between fighting constantly and having a great relationship? When you're reframing at this stage in the coaching, you're going to use the six human needs to understand is there another way for your client to meet the need behind the present goal right now? So if the goal is to make money, the need would be certainty. So how can they make sure to meet their need for certainty every day even before they make more money? Understanding the client's strengths versus the client's interests. So sometimes people think that their strengths are their interests and they don't really understand what their strengths are. So you want to get detailed about what is special about that person, what would their friends say about them. Building up just a bunch of information that the client has at their disposal to remind themselves of what makes themselves strong and special. And having that allows them to have a reframe which makes their goal or their outcome much more doable. So another example of this is the client says that their goal is to publish a book. And so the reframe would be that the client needs to write at least two pages every day. So the new goal is how is that person going to write two pages every day? It's shifted into a very doable daily goal. Great. And so the purpose of the second stage reframe when it comes to outcome-based coaching is that people will often have a general sense of urgency or an emotional ambition or excitement and it's very vague and it depends on things that are out of their control. Like I'm going to be a best-selling author. Well a lot of things have to happen in the world for you to become a best-selling author. So as a coach you want to bring them back to actionable steps with phases that they can meet their goal and outcome in parts every day. And the reframe is one of the ways that you want to help the person really assess what action is going to help take them there. And with action-based coaching there's a lot more focus on results in the world and so you kind of want to help to rein them in and help them do stuff that's in their control. And here the third is action which is what SI strategies you're going to use. We've talked a bit about all of these in different classes but just to run through them quickly if you're listening and not looking swallow the frog that's doing something first thing in the morning getting the hardest thing done right away that you need to do towards your outcome. Reverse engineering which Mark talked about using the example of Paris. Memory modification so overcoming any negative thoughts or memories that the client relives as an excuse to not do what they need to do. This can happen as you get into the details and the belief that your client can meet their goal they will usually come up with a list for you of all the reasons they can't. The things they think about, the things they remember, their rules, their beliefs. So you want to change that by confusing the thoughts and memories and you can do that in a fun way. I think we've given you examples using silly voices and black and white images and moving quickly or very slowly so that you go back into those thoughts and memories and you create a shift and they really can't go back there. You do that in a session and you have them practice that and they won't have those negative incantations or memories or beliefs anymore. Rewards, we really really believe in rewards so you want to help your client have small rewards every day for the small actions they take towards their goal. So the other thing is again because it's outcome based so it depends on the world and participation and understanding how things work. Another strategy is to have them interview other people who have attained similar goals or outcomes. It's important for your client to be getting good information to have good relationships with people who are achieving or involved with the types of achievements that they want for themselves. So again when people are outcome oriented they want to achieve something, it's important that they're not alone. So you can help them mobilize with people in their lives. So you want to expand this just not about you because one thing about when we make something just about us it becomes prone to secondary gain. It becomes just about us and our brain and meeting our own needs. When other people are involved we tend to get more organized and more responsible and we feel more accountable. The other thing is to help the person to get a mentor or a growth community that's specialized in the type of achievement that they want. So I always thought that was so funny when I read that in one of his books. But it's an easy thing to remember that you want to be around the people who have that thing that you're going towards. So if you want to be a runner, go join a group of runners and hear their stories. You want to be an artist, find a community of artists. So the other parts of expanding the unit is that you can help the person get an accountability partner. This could be you or it could be someone else who they can report to.
